question_data.text: The offence of Attempt is primarily criminalized to address:
options: The abandonment of a criminal enterprise.
Conduct that manifests an intention to commit a crime.
The legal impossibility of committing the complete crime.
question_data.explanation: Attempt targets steps taken toward a crime, even if the result remains unfulfilled.

question_data.text: For a defendant to be guilty of an attempt to commit a crime, the prosecution must establish:
options: The consequence of the completed offence.
An intentional act, regardless of the target crime.
Intent to commit the complete crime.
question_data.explanation: The mens rea for attempt is the specific intent to complete the principal offence.

question_data.text: According to R v Albert, what specific intent is required to convict for attempted murder?
options: Intent to cause Grievous Bodily Harm (GBH).
Intent to cause GBH or death.
Recklessness as to whether death would occur.
question_data.explanation: Attempted murder requires a higher standard of intent (to kill) than murder itself (which can be GBH).

question_data.text: Why can a defendant NOT be guilty of attempting to commit a crime which can only be committed recklessly or negligently?
options: Recklessness and negligence are irrelevant to criminal law.
The actus reus for those crimes is incomplete.
question_data.explanation: Attempt by definition involves a conscious goal to achieve a result, which is incompatible with recklessness.

question_data.text: Which element of the actus reus of attempt requires that the defendant has not fulfilled his intention to such an extent as to commit the offence?
options: Manifestation of intention by some overt act.
question_data.explanation: The 'commencement' stage marks where the action has moved beyond mere preparation but isn't yet complete.

question_data.text: The fundamental problem in the actus reus of attempt is to determine the distinction between an attempt and:
question_data.explanation: Courts must decide where innocent preparation ends and criminal attempt begins.

question_data.text: The Proximity Test for attempt, formulated in R v Eagleton, states that acts that are indictable are those:
options: Remotely leading towards the commission of an offence.
Immediately connected with the commission of an offence.
That only indicate a definite crime beyond reasonable doubt.
question_data.explanation: Proximity requires the act to be the 'last act' or very close to the final crime.

question_data.text: The objection raised against the Proximity Test is that:
options: It requires proof of an evil state of mind.
It is vague as there is no indication when acts pass from remote to immediate.
It is too favorable to the prosecution.
It does not consider the defendant's intention.
question_data.explanation: Proximity is often criticized for being subjective and inconsistent in application.

question_data.text: Which test for attempt is also known as the cinematographic or equivocality test, and requires the acts to 'indicate only' that the defendant intended to commit a certain definite crime?
question_data.explanation: The Turner test (equivocality) suggests an attempt is only proven if the act itself speaks to the criminal intent.

question_data.text: Okonkwo and Naish submitted that the draftsman of Section 4 of the Criminal Code (on attempt) probably had which theory in mind?
question_data.explanation: Nigerian scholars suggest the statutory definition aligns closely with the equivocality test.

question_data.text: Physical impossibility in the law of attempt means:
options: The defendant believes the act is illegal, but it is not.
It is physically impossible for the defendant to commit the complete crime whatever means he adopts.
The defendant voluntarily abandons the crime.
The defendant is inept, inefficient, or adopted insufficient means.
question_data.explanation: Example: Shooting at a person who isn't there.

question_data.text: In the context of attempt under Nigerian law, where there is a physical impossibility to commit the complete crime:
options: The charge is reduced to a simple offence.
The mens rea requirement is waived.
question_data.explanation: Section 4 of the Criminal Code explicitly states physical impossibility does not prevent a conviction for attempt.

question_data.text: In the context of attempt under Nigerian law, where there is a legal impossibility to commit the complete crime:
options: The actus reus is considered complete.
question_data.explanation: If the intended act is not actually a crime in law, attempting it is not criminal.

question_data.text: The case of Haughton v Smith (pre-1981 English law) established the rule that:
options: There is no offence of attempt where it is legally impossible to commit the offence.
The Proximity Test must be strictly applied.
Attempted crimes are crimes of strict liability.
question_data.explanation: Haughton v. Smith confirmed the common law principle regarding legal impossibility.

question_data.text: If a defendant has reached the stage of an attempt, what is the effect of the defendant voluntarily abandoning his criminal enterprise?
options: It serves as a complete defence.
It is irrelevant to liability, but may be relevant to sentencing.
It means the actus reus was never completed.
It means the mens rea was withdrawn.
question_data.explanation: Once the threshold for an attempt is crossed, abandonment is not a legal defence to the attempt itself.

question_data.text: The case of R v Shivpuri applied the English Criminal Attempts Act 1981 to convict a defendant of attempt where the defendant believed he was dealing with prohibited drugs, but the substance:
options: Was not in fact a prohibited drug.
question_data.explanation: Shivpuri overruled Haughton v Smith in England, holding that believing the act is a crime is sufficient.

question_data.text: Section 4 of the Criminal Code requires an offender, for attempt, to have begun to put his intention into execution by means adapted to its fulfillment, and:
options: Must have fulfilled his intention to commit the offence.
Must manifest his intention by some overt act.
Must only possess the necessary tools.
question_data.explanation: The statute requires an overt act as proof of the attempt.

question_data.text: The immaterialities under the second paragraph of Section 4 of the Criminal Code deal with factors that do not prevent a conviction for attempt, including:
options: Physical impossibility and incompletion independent of the perpetrator’s will.
question_data.explanation: If the attempt is thwarted or fails physically, the defendant is still liable.

question_data.text: In the context of the elements of an offence, attempted crimes (like rape or murder) require:
options: Some conduct to manifest the evil state of mind.
A lesser mens rea than the completed crime.
Only an evil state of mind.
question_data.explanation: Conduct is always required for criminal responsibility.

question_data.text: Conspiracy to murder (Section 324 CC) is listed as an offence punishable by imprisonment for:
question_data.explanation: Conspiracy to commit murder is a major felony.

question_data.text: Preparation to commit offences, which includes conspiracy and attempts, is classified under which part of the Criminal Code?
question_data.explanation: Inchoate offences are grouped in Part 8 of the Code.

question_data.text: In the case of Haughton v Smith (1975), the court held that there is a distinction between:
options: The intention to commit a crime and an attempt to commit it.
question_data.explanation: Intention alone is not an attempt; an overt act bordering on completion is required.

question_data.text: Which of the following is listed as an exception where preparation to commit an offence is explicitly addressed in the Criminal Code?
question_data.explanation: Forging is one of the few areas where mere preparation of instruments can be criminal.

question_data.text: The definition of an Indictable offence is relevant to attempt because, as a felony, it informs:
options: The procedures for arrest and bail.
question_data.explanation: Seriousness of the attempt matches the principal offence for procedural purposes.

question_data.text: Which classification category is also referred to as 'preliminary offences'?
question_data.explanation: Preliminary or inchoate offences include attempts and conspiracies.

question_data.text: The mens rea for attempt focuses on the defendant's:
options: Foresight and desire for the consequence of the complete crime.
question_data.explanation: Attempt requires the specific desire to achieve the criminal result.

question_data.text: The case of R v Ring is an example that demonstrates that in cases of physical impossibility:
question_data.explanation: R v Ring involved attempting to pick an empty pocket.

question_data.text: The actus reus of attempt requires the offender to have taken steps towards the offence. The real problem lies in determining:
options: The specific stage at which the accused can be held liable for attempting.
The severity of the intended harm.
question_data.explanation: The 'threshold' of attempt is the most litigated part of the doctrine.

question_data.text: The Equivocality Test requires the steps taken by the accused to have reached the point when they indicate:
options: A reasonable doubt as to the end to which they were directed.
Beyond reasonable doubt what was the end to which they were directed.
That the crime was physically impossible.
That the crime was legally impossible.
question_data.explanation: If the act alone doesn't prove the intent, it's 'equivocal' and potentially not an attempt.

question_data.text: The maximum imprisonment for Written threats to murder (Section 323) is:
question_data.explanation: Section 323 of the CC sets 7 years for this felony.
